text: AAS to support the Operator as a Service vision Ander Garcia Vicomtech Foundation, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA) Donostia-San Sebastian, Spain 0000-0001-5596-2838 Sergio Valdés Vicomtech Foundation, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA) Donostia-San Sebastian, Spain Xabier Oregui Vicomtech Foundation, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA) Donostia-San Sebastian, Spain Jairo R. Sánchez Vicomtech Foundation, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA) Donostia-San Sebastian, Spain Marco Ojer Vicomtech Foundation, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA) Donostia-San Sebastian, Spain Iñigo Mendizabal Vicomtech Foundation, Basque Research and Technology Alliance (BRTA) Donostia-San Sebastian, Spain Abstract— The Operator as a Service (OaaS) vision is a novel paradigm where skilled remote operators are empowered with advanced digital technologies based on digital twins to remotely oversee and control multiple autonomous and flexible smart manufacturing lines. This paper proposes the application of Asset Administration Shell (AAS) to support a scalable deployment of the OaaS vision. Remote operators and flexible manufacturing lines are connected through an AAS dataspace, which provides connectivity and security and traceability functionalities. Furthermore, both operators and manufacturing lines can control with whom they collaborate. Once remote operators and manufacturing lines are connected, every time an unexpected situation occurs, the remote operator receives a notification to connect to the digital twin of the line to evaluate the situation and to solve it. This approach has been implemented based on Eclipse Basyx and AAS Dataspace for Everybody, extending an existing autonomous assembly line testbed and successfully validating it during the 2025 edition of the Hannover Messe. Keywords—AAS, Digital Twin, Remote Operator I. INTRODUCTION Industry 4.0 technologies, such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), digital twins and data analytics, have transformed shop floors, impacting the role of traditional operators and leading to Operators 4.0. Operator 4.0 [1] represents the next generation of workforce in manufacturing, equipped with advanced digital competencies and the ability to interact seamlessly with smart machines. As the automation level of manufacturing lines increases, the role of these Operators 4.0 shifts from performing manual task to applying their experience and knowledge to (i) monitor production machines, (ii) react to and solve unexpected circumstances, (iii) perform maintenance operations or (iv) control very specific steps of the production process. Even if these operators perform a passive monitoring through a relevant time of their work period, their presence is still required full time to avoid stopping the manufacturing line. On a recent paper [2] we introduced the novel Operator as a Service (OaaS) vision. In the OaaS vision, a skilled remote operator empowered with extended interaction technologies can oversee and control multiple automated manufacturing lines. The OaaS concept leverages the capabilities of Operator 4.0 to facilitate the transition to lights-out manufacturing. However, instead of empowered full-time on-premise operators, the OaaS vision is based on remote specialized operators available on demand when the automated lines face an unexpected situation or an error. Consequently, the OaaS concept represents a step towards fully automatized factories, where manufacturing plants could be completely automated and specialized remote human labour would be required on demand to analyse and react to unexpected situations. Thus, the operators are mainly required to perform surveillance tasks, to detect failures or unexpected events that may halt the production line or provoke severe failures. On the previous paper [2], we proposed the vision, an architecture, and we presented an OaaS testbed. On that architecture, manufacturing lines and remote operators were directly connected, making it unfeasible for real scenarios where several autonomous factories (N) would be supervised by several remote operators (M), as this would require managing and securing NxM connections. This paper focuses on the application of the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) to scale the OaaS. In this paper we propose an architecture where manufacturing lines and remote operators publish their digital twin as AASs into an AAS dataspace. Lines and remote operators are connected through their AASs at the dataspace, scaling the OaaS vision to scenarios composed of several manufacturing lines and remote operators. The architecture of the previous testbed has been upgraded to integrate AAS and it has been successfully validated. This paper is structured as follows. Section 2 presents the related state of the art. Section 3 summarises the main concepts of the OaaS vision and the testbed. Section 4 focuses on the new architecture and the extended testbed to integrate the AAS. Finally, while section 5 describes the validation, section 6 highlights the conclusions and the future work. II. STATE OF THE ART While [2] analyses the state of the art related to remote operations and the OaaS core concepts, this paper focuses on the AAS and industrial dataspaces Asset Administration Shells (AAS) are key for advanced digitalization development in Industry 4.0 [3]. They function as standardized digital representations of physical assets, encapsulating technical, operational, and lifecycle information in a "digital wrapper." This allows different systems, devices, and platforms to communicate and interoperate, eliminating traditional information silos. While AAS is designed to improve interoperability, information access, and storage standardization in industrial environments, its adoption still faces significant challenges. According to [4], some of these challenges include the difficulty of integration with pre-existing industrial systems, the need to develop appropriate data sovereignty and governance models, and the complexity of ensuring traceability in complex industrial scenarios. These challenges underscore the importance of continuing to advance standardization, the development of collaborative systems, and solutions that improve automation and security in AAS management. There are several examples of AAS integration into industrial scenarios. Singh et al. [5] describe the implementation of AAS in modular production environments, where the ability of AAS to integrate real-time operational data improves process flexibility and efficiency. Danish and Schnicke [6] demonstrate the digitization of components in minifactories using AAS, enabling standardized and dynamic orchestration of production processes. Furthermore, Sylvestre and Dhouib [7] explore how transforming data from legacy systems (such as ERP and PLM) into AAS formats enables interoperability between diverse systems and facilitates the integration of critical information for real-time decision- making. Regarding data spaces, they are infrastructures that enable the secure and controlled exchange of information between companies and institutions, guaranteeing the sovereignty and privacy of shared data. These spaces facilitate collaboration between entities, enabling the development of new data- driven products and services, which contributes to economic growth and social well-being. In this context, the combination of data spaces with Asset Administration Shells (AAS) is emerging as a key solution for interoperability and secure data exchange in Industry 4.0 [8]. While AAS provide a standardized digital representation of assets, dataspaces facilitate the controlled and sovereign exchange of information between companies. However, the integration of AAS into dataspaces faces challenges such as a lack of standardization, data governance, and integration with legacy systems, which hinders large-scale adoption [7]. To address these challenges, initiatives such as the Eclipse Dataspace Connector (EDC) have emerged, which enables data exchange under access policies defined by information owners [9], and GAIA-X based approaches have also been proposed [10]. Also noteworthy is the BaSyx DataBridge, a solution that facilitates the connection of legacy systems with AAS through protocols such as OPC UA, improving integration with existing infrastructures [11]. Despite advances in data spaces and AAS connectivity tools with industrial assets, the integration of AAS as an entity in its own right, not as an exchange of static data, remains crucial to enable its sharing and exploitation. Furthermore, few scenarios for applications of AAS and dataspaces have been proposed. This paper tackles these aspects, applying AAS and dataspace as key elements to scale the OaaS vision. III. OAAS VISION This section summarises the OaaS vision [2], where remote operators intervene on demand. This optimizes human resources allocation as the remote operator can perform unrelated tasks as long as there are no error notifications. Besides, one remote operator can take care of multiple processes. These processes can take place in the same factory or across different plants. Figure 1 General OaaS workflow for remote operators [2] Figure 1 shows the general workflow of remote operators. Once a notification is received, the remote operator opens the OaaS application to understand the problem at the manufacturing line with its digital twin. Then, in case of false negatives, the operator confirms it and commands the line to continue production. Otherwise, the operator defines a strategy to solve the problem. If the problem can be solved, the solution is planned at the digital twin before ordering its execution and remotely verifying the problem is solved. This cycle can be repeated until the problem is solved. For cases where no remote solution can be planned, the operator can ask for on-site support. The technological validation of this vision was based on a testbed of a the fully automated assembly of a small-scale 3D printed car consisting of a chassis, four wheels, and four screws (Figure 2). Figure 2 Chassis, wheels and screws of the testbed car The assembly line (Figure 3) is composed of a KUKA IIWA 14, a collaborative robot with 7 degrees of freedom. Its end effector includes a PGC140 gripper and an Alvium 1800 U-507 camera. The PGC140 gripper is a parallel electric gripper with gripping feedback and configurable gripping force and velocity and the Alvium 1800 is a 5.1 Megapixels RGB camera. The gripper fingers have been designed and printed for this specific application. The input pieces (wheels and chassis) are placed within a tray, and the screws are arranged on a custom feeder so the robot can go to a predefined picking position. There is also an assembly area that holds the car during the mounting process and a flip area that helps the robot with the car flipping operation, With this setup, the robot has to mount a car and leave it in an output tray. The video of the complete automatic mounting process is available for interested readers [12]. Figure 3 Assembly line of the testbed For the remote operator side, Unity 3D is used to build a digital twin of the manufacturing process. Note that the digital twin’s behaviour, including real-world synchronization and bidirectional communication, is limited to the robotic asset. Changes in the state of physical objects do not affect the virtual scene, with the exception of the robotic system (robot and gripper). Detection and registration of other elements in the real environment would be a demanding task and would add complexity to the process. The Graphical User Interface (GUI) includes various panels to perform different actions and visualize operational information, alongside the 3D representation of the scene (Figure 4). The communication between the line and the remote operator was based on OPC UA. The line had an OPC UA server and the remote operator connected to this server through an OPC UA client. This server acted as the real-time data monitor by exposing state variables and information about the hardware components, such as robot joint positions, gripper width, and camera image. It also acted as the command executor, performing actions on the robot-gripper system based on commands received via OPC UA from the client side. Figure 4 GUI of the remote operator While this testbed successfully validated the viability and applicability of the OaaS vision, its architecture presented a relevant scalability drawback. In scenarios with N manufacturing lines and M remote operators, each line would have to manage M connections, and each remote operator N connections, making the vision unfeasible to scale. This paper targets this drawback managing the communication with an AAS dataspace connecting manufacturing lines with remote operators. IV. AAS INTEGRATION This section presents an architecture to integrate AAS into the OaaS vision and describes the extension of the OaaS testbed with AAS. In the AAS based architecture, each factory has one or more AAS based digital twins of one or several manufacturing lines. Each of these AAS is published at the AAS Dataspace. In parallel, each remote operator, who also has a OaaS AAS, publishes its AAS at the dataspace. After this publication step, factory lines and remote operators have to reach agreements (online or offline) and grant access to each other AASs to connect. Once this connection step is over, each time an autonomous line detects an unexpected situation, the digital twin of the line sends a notification to the remote operator using the AAS REST API. This notification is received by the AAS of the OaaS, which sends it to the application through Eclipse Databridge to be shown to the remote operator. The remote operator receives the notification, and starts using the OaaS application. The OaaS application communicates with the AAS of the remote line using the AAS Rest API to evaluate the situation, fix any possible problem and restart the autonomous production. Communication between the AAS and the real assets (lines, operators) is managed with Eclipse Basyx Databridge. While each real asset may select any technology compatible with Databridge to communicate with its AAS, we propose OPC UA for remote lines, where Databridge maps the OPC UA variables and methods of the assets with the AAS at the dataspace. For the OaaS application, any technology (MQTT, REST API, OPC UA, Kafka,…) supported by the application and Databridge could be used. Figure 5 summarises this architecture. The proposed AAS architecture scales the OaaS vision to scenarios with several manufacturing lines and remote operators. Now, each line or operator only must manage the connection to the AAS dataspace. Furthermore, security and traceability functionalities of the dataspace fulfil requirements of most industrial use case scenarios. Focusing on the testbed, first an AAS of the autonomous line has been generated. This AAS has a submodel modelling main variables and methods of the autonomous manufacturing line. For the validation this submodel resembles the structure of the OPC UA server of the line. The AAS has been generated with the Eclipse AASX Package Explorer. The complete AAS is accessible at the dataspace and its main components of the submodel are the following: • operation_mode, for differentiate manual and automic mode of the line • operation_status, representing the current status of the manufacturing workflow • car_face, representing which face of the chassis is being assembled • bolt_assembled, counting the number of bolts already assembled on a car • wheel_assembled, counting the number of wheels already assembled on a car • set_operation_status, to override the status of the manufacturing workflow by the remote operator • photo_command, to command the robot to take a photo • raw_photo, to access the photo taken by the robot • robot_joints, with information about the position of each joint • gripper_position, representing the position of the gripper The AAS of remote operators is composed by the properties required to receive error notifications from remote lines. In order to keep the compatibility with the existing OaaS application, the remote operator of the testbed also has an OPC UA server. Both AAS have been uploaded to the AAS dataspace. The AAS dataspace is based on AAS Dataspace for Everybody and it is being hosted by Fraunhofer IESE [13]. Finally, OPC UA servers of the manufacturing line and the previous OaaS applications have been connected to their AASs configuring the mapping of each method and variable with Eclipse Basyx Databridge. This configuration is based on JSON files where each OPC UA variable is linked to an endpoint of the submodel. Figure 6 shows a subset of this configuration as an example. Figure 6 Example of the Databridge configuration Figure 5 Proposed AAS OaaS architecture V. VALIDATION The validation of the proposed AAS OaaS architecture has started with the validation of the technical viability of the extension of the previous testbed. This AAS extension of the tested has faced two main problems related to current data communication capabilities through AAS and Eclipse Basyx. The first problem is related to transmission of images of the factory line as binary data to the remote operator through the AAS. As the current Basyx and Databridge implementation lacks support for binary data, images have been sent as base64 encoded strings. This support is expected for future releases. The second problem has been the data update frequency. Although data updates of 1-2 seconds are correct for non- critical value updates, the digital 3D representation of elements such as joints from robots requires a higher update frequency to avoid a non-continuous movement representation. While the real-time data transmission through Basyx improves, in order to maintain a fluent 3D representation at the OaaS application, we have been forced to roll back to include a direct connection to the OPC UA server of the robot of the manufacturing line from the OaaS application. Using the connection, the OaaS subscribes to the joints and receives a fluent real-time update of the joints, providing a proper visualization and interaction of the OaaS application. Once the real-time data communication through AAS Dataspace and Eclipse Basyx is available, the full implementation of the proposed architecture will be performed. After this technical validation, the AAS testbed has been presented at the 2025 edition of the Hannover Messe within the Fraunhoffer IESE stand (Figure 8). AASs of the autonomous line has been published at the AAS Dataspace for Everybody managed by Fraunhofer IESE (Figure 7). Figure 7 Sample of the AAS at the dataspace GUI While the autonomous line has stayed at the laboratory from Vicomtech in Spain, the remote operators and the OaaS application have been in Hannover for the whole week. Within this week, each time an unexpected situation happened at the manufacturing line, a notification was received at Hannover and the issue was resolved using the OaaS application. In order to ease the explanation of the OaaS vision, two remote cameras were also installed at Vicomtech. As the remote operator can send commands to take photographs of the line, these cameras are not required by the remote operator. However, seeing the 3D representation of the digital twin of the line inside the remote OaaS application together with the real-time feed from the webcams, make it clearer the OaaS vision and the potential of the testbed. The screen of the stand (Figure 8) has been split in 3 parts. The main one (2/3) has been assigned to the OaaS application, while the remaining third has been assigned to the two remote cameras. Figure 8 Detail of the Hannover Messe stand VI. CONCLUSSIONS AND FUTURE WORK The Operator as a Service (OaaS) vision is a novel vision where several autonomous factories are supervised on- demand by several remote operators empowered with Industry 4.0 technologies such as AI and digital twins. While the technical viability of this vision has been previously validated, the previously proposed architecture only tackled scenarios with a few lines and operators, not being able to scale. This paper proposes to integrate AAS in the OaaS vision to overcome this drawback. First, an architecture based on an AAS dataspace is proposed. In this architecture, communication between manufacturing lines and remote operators is managed through an AAS Dataspace. The dataspace manages AAS of the lines and the remote operators. Each line an operator executes an OPC UA Server that based on Eclipse Basyx Databridge connects the AAS with the physical asset. The AAS REST API is the interface to communicate between lines and remote operators. This architecture scales the OaaS vision, and provides relevant security and traceability functionalities for real scenarios. In order to validate the architecture, the existing testbed has been extended. This validation has shown that some AAS functionalities are still not mature enough, mainly the real- time data communication. As real-time data is required for a fluent 3D interaction, the extended testbed has been forced to implement a direct communication to overcome this limitation. The extended testbed has been presented and validated during the Hannover Messe 2025 edition at the Fraunhofer IESE stand. The validation has proven that AAS solves scalability problems of the previous OaaS architecture and that it is a key aspect for future deployments of the OaaS vision. Regarding the planned future work, the main one tackles connectivity issues with Basyx for binary data and the integration of real-time data sources, such as MQTT brokers to fully implement the proposed architecture, even for data about the joints of the robot. The second line focuses on standardizing the submodels of the autonomous lines and the remote operators to increase the interoperability of the OaaS vision with several factories and different manufacturing lines. The third line focuses on applying the OaaS vision to more manufacturing scenarios with the objective to validate it in a controlled environment but within a real manufacturing shopfloor. ACKNOWLEDGMENT This research was partially funded by the dAIEDGE project (HORIZON-CL4-2022-HUMAN-02-02, Grant Agreement Number: 101120726) and by the GIZAKITU project (KK-2025/00035 Elkartek program of the Basque Government).
